Moon Over Causeway Bay: Your 2026 Mid-Autumn Festival Guide

With the Mid-Autumn Festival falling on Friday, 25 September 2026, Causeway Bay places both of the season's great traditions within a short stroll: the lantern carnival glowing across Victoria Park and the incense-lit Tai Hang Fire Dragon Dance. This guide maps the celebration — from family feasting at PLAYT Buffet to moon-viewing at SKYE Roofbar & Brasserie — and shows why The Park Lane Hong Kong, Autograph Collection — a proud member of the Marriott Bonvoy portfolio, directly opposite Victoria Park — is the most effortless base for the festival.

Table of Contents

*   [Victoria Park Lantern Carnival: Right Across the Road](https://parklane.com.hk/en/hong-kong-mid-autumn-festival-causeway-bay-guide#lantern-carnival)
*   [Tai Hang Fire Dragon Dance: A Living Heritage Nearby](https://parklane.com.hk/en/hong-kong-mid-autumn-festival-causeway-bay-guide#fire-dragon)
*   [Festive Dining at The Park Lane Hong Kong, Autograph Collection](https://parklane.com.hk/en/hong-kong-mid-autumn-festival-causeway-bay-guide#festive-dining)
*   [Moon-Viewing Above the Harbour at SKYE Roofbar & Brasserie](https://parklane.com.hk/en/hong-kong-mid-autumn-festival-causeway-bay-guide#moon-viewing)
*   [A Mid-Autumn Staycation for the Whole Family](https://parklane.com.hk/en/hong-kong-mid-autumn-festival-causeway-bay-guide#staycation)

When the harvest moon rises over Victoria Harbour, Causeway Bay becomes the most atmospheric corner of the city to mark the Mid-Autumn Festival. With the festival falling on Friday, 25 September 2026, the district's two great traditions — the lantern carnival glowing across Victoria Park and the incense-lit Tai Hang Fire Dragon Dance weaving through the back streets — sit within a short stroll of one another. This guide maps the celebration and shows why The Park Lane Hong Kong, Autograph Collection — a proud member of the Marriott Bonvoy portfolio, directly opposite Victoria Park — is the most effortless base for the season.

[](https://parklane.com.hk/en/hong-kong-mid-autumn-festival-causeway-bay-guide)
## Mid-Autumn 2026 at a Glance: Dates & Timing

The Mid-Autumn Festival follows the lunar calendar, so its date shifts each year. Here is what you need to plan the 2026 celebration around Causeway Bay.

*   **The Festival Date — Friday, 25 September 2026:** Mid-Autumn falls on the 15th night of the eighth lunar month, when the full moon is at its brightest. In 2026, that lands on Friday, 25 September, with the following day — Saturday, 26 September — observed as the public holiday, giving families a natural long weekend.
*   **The Best Time to Be Out:** The festival is an evening celebration. Lanterns, moon-viewing, and the fire dragon all come alive after dark, so plan a relaxed daytime and save your energy for the night. Book dinner early — this is one of the busiest evenings on the Hong Kong dining calendar.
*   **Why Causeway Bay:** Few districts place the festival's two headline traditions so close together. From The Park Lane Hong Kong, Autograph Collection, Victoria Park's lantern displays are directly across the road, and Tai Hang's fire dragon is a short walk away — no cross-harbour travel, no long taxi queues.

[](https://parklane.com.hk/en/hong-kong-mid-autumn-festival-causeway-bay-guide)
## Victoria Park Lantern Carnival: Right Across the Road

Causeway Bay's Victoria Park hosts the city's largest Mid-Autumn lantern carnival, and it sits directly opposite the hotel — arguably the single best reason to base yourself here for the festival.

*   **[Victoria Park](https://www.lcsd.gov.hk/en/parks/vp/) – The City's Largest Carnival:** Organised by the Leisure and Cultural Services Department around the Mid-Autumn period, the Victoria Park carnival fills the lawns with large themed lantern installations, traditional craft, and cultural performances. Its scale and its position on the harbour side make it the district's centrepiece — and it is free to enjoy.
*   **[The Mid-Autumn Experience](https://www.discoverhongkong.com/eng/events/mid-autumn-festival.html) – Lanterns & Tradition:** Expect glowing lantern tunnels, Chinese opera and folk performances, and families carrying handmade and character lanterns. Confirm the exact carnival dates and programme on the official listing closer to the festival, as the schedule is announced each year.
*   **Visitor Tip:** Arrive after sunset for the full effect, when the lanterns are lit and the park is at its most photogenic. Because you are staying directly across the road, you can wander over at leisure and return whenever you like — a real advantage with children in tow.

[](https://parklane.com.hk/en/hong-kong-mid-autumn-festival-causeway-bay-guide)
## Tai Hang Fire Dragon Dance: A Living Heritage Nearby

A short walk inland from Causeway Bay, the neighbourhood of Tai Hang stages one of Hong Kong's most spectacular living traditions — a performance recognised as a national intangible cultural heritage.

*   **[The Tai Hang Fire Dragon Dance](https://www.discoverhongkong.com/eng/events/tai-hang-s-fire-dragon-dance.html) – A 140-Year Tradition:** A dragon around 67 metres long, its body studded with thousands of glowing incense sticks, is carried through Tai Hang's narrow streets by hundreds of performers to the beat of drums. The custom dates back more than a century and is traditionally performed over three consecutive nights around the Mid-Autumn Festival.
*   **Where & When to Watch:** The dance winds through the lanes around Wun Sha Street and Tung Lo Wan Road in Tai Hang, a short walk from the hotel. Check the official listing for the confirmed 2026 performance nights and start times, then arrive early to secure a good vantage point — the streets fill quickly.
*   **Visitor Tip:** The atmosphere is smoky and crowded in the best possible way. Wear comfortable shoes, keep valuables secure, and consider watching from the edge of the route for a clearer view. Afterwards, Tai Hang's cafés and the walk back to Causeway Bay make for an easy wind-down.

[](https://parklane.com.hk/en/hong-kong-mid-autumn-festival-causeway-bay-guide)
## Festive Dining at The Park Lane Hong Kong, Autograph Collection

Mid-Autumn is, at heart, a festival of reunion and shared food. After the lanterns and the dragon, gather the family for a celebratory meal at The Park Lane Hong Kong, Autograph Collection, where world-class Marriott Bonvoy hospitality meets the warmth of a Causeway Bay icon.

*   **[PLAYT Buffet](https://www.parklane.com.hk/en/playt) – The Family Reunion Feast:** For multi-generational gatherings, PLAYT Buffet is the natural choice. The all-day buffet pairs open-kitchen theatre with industrial-chic interiors and a generous seafood bar of fresh lobster, crab legs, and seasonal seafood — an easy way to please grandparents, parents, and children at one table. Book direct for up to 24% off, and reserve early for the festival weekend.
*   **[Ebb & Flow](https://www.parklane.com.hk/en/ebb-n-flow) – Tea, Coffee & a Quieter Toast:** For a gentler pace, the lobby lounge Ebb & Flow is ideal for an afternoon coffee before the evening's festivities or a relaxed nightcap once the family returns from the park.
*   **Mooncakes & the Season:** Mooncakes are the edible heart of Mid-Autumn — shared among family and offered as gifts. Look out for the hotel's seasonal offerings closer to the festival, and confirm any mooncake or set-menu details directly when you book.

[](https://parklane.com.hk/en/hong-kong-mid-autumn-festival-causeway-bay-guide)
## Moon-Viewing Above the Harbour at SKYE Roofbar & Brasserie

The full moon is the whole point of the festival — and there are few finer places to admire it than 27 floors above Causeway Bay at **[SKYE Roofbar & Brasserie](https://www.parklane.com.hk/en/skye)**.

SKYE Roofbar & Brasserie is Hong Kong's stylish rooftop cocktail bar and restaurant, where authentic French cuisine under Chef de Cuisine Florian Muller meets sweeping views of Victoria Harbour and Victoria Park. On Mid-Autumn night, the open-air terrace offers an unobstructed view of the rising moon — a modern, elegant take on a very old tradition.

*   **[A Toast Under the Moon](https://www.parklane.com.hk/en/skye) – The Hong Kong Streets Series:** Raise the signature "Gloucester Road" cocktail — built on PL50, the hotel's exclusive gin, and named for the street The Park Lane Hong Kong, Autograph Collection calls home. It is a fitting way to toast the harvest moon over the harbour.
*   **Dress Code & Reservations:** SKYE Roofbar & Brasserie maintains a smart-casual dress code (no flip-flops, singlets, or indoor slippers). Mid-Autumn is one of the busiest rooftop evenings of the year, so terrace seats are best reserved well in advance.

[](https://parklane.com.hk/en/hong-kong-mid-autumn-festival-causeway-bay-guide)
## A Mid-Autumn Staycation for the Whole Family

With the public holiday falling on Saturday, 26 September, Mid-Autumn 2026 is a ready-made long weekend. Turn the celebration into a full staycation by booking a Premium Room or Suite at The Park Lane Hong Kong, Autograph Collection — and let Victoria Park's lanterns become the view from your window.

Wake to floor-to-ceiling views of the park and harbour, and enjoy the festival at your own pace with the lantern carnival and the fire dragon both within easy reach. Executive Lounge access is available with select stay packages. As a Marriott Bonvoy member, eligible stays currently earn bonus points that add meaningful value to an already memorable weekend — confirm the current offer and dates when you book.

For multi-generational reunions, the property's Family Room sleeps up to four and currently includes an HK$300 hotel dining credit — ideal for a festival built around gathering the whole family under one roof, without the hassle of separate cars and separate bookings.

[](https://parklane.com.hk/en/hong-kong-mid-autumn-festival-causeway-bay-guide)
## Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ) for Mid-Autumn 2026

*   **When is the Mid-Autumn Festival in Hong Kong in 2026?**

The Mid-Autumn Festival falls on Friday, 25 September 2026, with the following day, Saturday, 26 September, observed as a public holiday. It is an evening celebration, so plan your lantern-viewing and dinner for after dark and book restaurants well in advance.
*   **Where is the best Mid-Autumn lantern carnival in Causeway Bay?**

Victoria Park hosts the city's largest Mid-Autumn lantern carnival, directly across the road from [The Park Lane Hong Kong, Autograph Collection](https://www.parklane.com.hk/en/accommodations). It is free to attend; confirm the exact dates and programme on the official listing closer to the festival.
*   **How close is the Tai Hang Fire Dragon Dance to the hotel?**

Tai Hang is a short walk inland from Causeway Bay, making The Park Lane Hong Kong, Autograph Collection a convenient base for watching the fire dragon. The dance is traditionally performed over three consecutive nights around Mid-Autumn; check the official listing for the confirmed 2026 nights and start times.
*   **Where can I enjoy a festive Mid-Autumn dinner or moon-viewing?**

For a family reunion feast, [PLAYT Buffet](https://www.parklane.com.hk/en/playt) offers open-kitchen energy and an extensive seafood bar, with up to 24% off when you book direct. For moon-viewing with a harbour panorama, [SKYE Roofbar & Brasserie](https://www.parklane.com.hk/en/skye) on the 27th floor pairs authentic French cuisine with an open-air terrace and the signature "Gloucester Road" cocktail built on the hotel's exclusive PL50 gin.

Get to the Night Market
Visitors and locals alike congregate in the evenings in Hong Kong's last remaining Night Market. The Night Market at Temple Street has been a fixture of the Jordan/Yau Ma Tei district since at least the 1920s and probably before.

Today it boasts market stalls with fashions and accessories, seafood and claypot rice in traditional Dai Pai Dong restaurants, fortunetellers and tarot card readers, and Cantonese Opera singers. Nowhere else in Hong Kong is such an interesting cross section of Hong Kong culture, cuisine, commerce and society to be found.

results.content:
Contact Us

25 September 2026

# Mid-Autumn Festival

The Mid-Autumn Festival, also known as the Moon Festival or Mooncake Festival, falls on the 15th day of the eighth lunar month and is an occasion for families to reunite and have a good time together. In Hong Kong, that means sharing mooncakes with your loved ones, going moongazing and enjoying a range of festive events from lantern carnivals to the Tai Hang Fire Dragon Dance.

## Celebrate the Hong Kong Mid-Autumn Festival

Discover the cherished customs and festive treats.

### Witness the Tai Hang Fire Dragon Dance [...] ### Immerse in city-wide lantern displays

During Mid-Autumn, Hong Kong transforms into a lantern-filled wonderland. Across the city, spectacular lantern displays showcase imaginative shapes, from whimsical cartoon characters to elaborate floral motifs. The creativity is evident at Fuk Wing Street or Tai Kiu Market where small shops brim with an astounding variety of eye-catching lanterns. Embrace the celebratory spirit by parading your chosen lantern at the Lantern Carnivals throughout Hong Kong, including the popular celebration in Victoria Park. Alternatively, stroll along the 200-metre-long Lee Tung Avenue, where an illuminated canopy of lanterns create an enchanting festive atmosphere.

Find out more

 Tai Kiu market

  What are some events and activities for Mid-Autumn [...] ## Top locations for Mid-Autumn atmosphere

Places to immerse yourself in the festivities, from lantern shopping to carnivals.

 

  Victoria Park

  Causeway Bay’s Victoria Park plays host to the largest Mid-Autumn lantern carnival in town, with impressive lantern displays, Chinese opera performances and more.
 

  Lee Tung Avenue

  Lee Tung Avenue in Wan Chai is a popular spot for photo-taking during the Mid-Autumn Festival with its 200-metre-long walkway lit up by a colourful canopy of lanterns.
 

  Tai Hang Fire Dragon Heritage Centre

  This Grade III historic building houses a fascinating exhibition centre that showcases the history of the Tai Hang Fire Dragon Dance and Hakka culture of Tai Hang.
 

  Fuk Wing Street

# Mid-Autumn Festival

The Mid-Autumn Festival, also known as the Moon Festival or Mooncake Festival, falls on the 15th day of the eighth lunar month and is an occasion for families to reunite and have a good time together. In Hong Kong, that means sharing mooncakes with your loved ones, going moongazing and enjoying a range of festive events from lantern carnivals to the Tai Hang Fire Dragon Dance.

## Celebrate the Hong Kong Mid-Autumn Festival

Discover the cherished customs and festive treats.

![](/content/dam/dhk/intl/explore/culture/mid-autumn-festival/2024/mid-autumn-tai-hang-fire-dragoon-960x720.jpg)

### Witness the Tai Hang Fire Dragon Dance

Tai Hang becomes a hotspot during Mid-Autumn as locals and visitors gather to watch the captivating Tai Hang Fire Dragon Dance. Follow the 67-metre fire dragon made of incense sticks, paraded by 300 performers, with a small dragon carried by children, as it continues this tradition and winds through the streets.

Find out more

* [![Tai Hang Fire Dragon Dance](/content/dam/dhk/intl/explore/culture/mid-autumn-festival/2024/tai-hang-fire-dragon-960x720.jpg)

  Tai Hang Fire Dragon Dance](/eng/events/tai-hang-s-fire-dragon-dance.html)

![](/content/dam/dhk/intl/explore/culture/mid-autumn-festival/2024/moongaze-skyline-960x720.jpg)

### Moongaze amid Hong Kong’s skyline

Gazing at the full moon is an important custom during Mid-Autumn in Hong Kong, as it symbolises reunion. People gather at picturesque harbourfront parks to soak in the festive ambience, with Hong Kong’s iconic skyline forming a breathtaking backdrop against the moon. For a more unique experience, board a ferry to a remote island or take a harbour cruise to admire the city’s vistas under the glowing moon and fully embrace this time-honoured tradition.

Find out more

* [![Moongaze amid Hong Kong’s skyline](/content/dam/dhk/intl/explore/culture/mid-autumn-festival/2024/moongaze-skyline-pick-960x720.jpg)

  How do Hong Kong people celebrate Mid-Autumn](/eng/culture/how-to-celebrate-mid-autumn-festival-in-hong-kong.html#what-to-do)

![](/content/dam/dhk/intl/explore/culture/mid-autumn-festival/2024/mid-autumn-fish-960x720.jpg)

### Immerse in city-wide lantern displays

During Mid-Autumn, Hong Kong transforms into a lantern-filled wonderland. Across the city, spectacular lantern displays showcase imaginative shapes, from whimsical cartoon characters to elaborate floral motifs. The creativity is evident at Fuk Wing Street or Tai Kiu Market where small shops brim with an astounding variety of eye-catching lanterns. Embrace the celebratory spirit by parading your chosen lantern at the Lantern Carnivals throughout Hong Kong, including the popular celebration in Victoria Park. Alternatively, stroll along the 200-metre-long Lee Tung Avenue, where an illuminated canopy of lanterns create an enchanting festive atmosphere.

Find out more

* [![Tai Kiu market](/content/dam/dhk/intl/explore/culture/mid-autumn-festival/2024/mid-autumn-pick-tai-kiu-market-960x720.jpg)

  What are some events and activities for Mid-Autumn](/eng/culture/how-to-celebrate-mid-autumn-festival-in-hong-kong.html#what-to-do)

![](/content/dam/dhk/intl/explore/culture/mid-autumn-festival/2024/mid-autumn-moon-cake-960x720.jpg)

### Savour mooncakes and local delicacies

At the heart of Mid-Autumn celebrations in Hong Kong are the beloved mooncakes, which have evolved beyond the traditional lotus seed paste and salted egg yolk fillings. Hong Kong has given rise to countless varieties from the delicate snow skin mooncakes to indulgent egg custard renditions and even savoury flaky crusts with minced pork. Other quintessential festival delicacies include tong yuen (sweet glutinous rice balls) with delicious fillings like black sesame or peanut. Auspicious fruits like pomelo, pomegranate and star fruit are also festival staples.

Find out more

* [![Moon Cake](/content/dam/dhk/intl/explore/culture/mid-autumn-festival/2024/mid-autumn-moon-cake-pick-960x720.jpg)

  Indulge in Mid-Autumn delicacies](/eng/culture/how-to-celebrate-mid-autumn-festival-in-hong-kong.html#what-to-eat)

## Top locations for Mid-Autumn atmosphere

Places to immerse yourself in the festivities, from lantern shopping to carnivals.

* [![](/content/dam/dhk/intl/explore/neighbourhoods/interactive-map/attractions/victoria-park-640-480.jpg)

  Victoria Park

  Causeway Bay’s Victoria Park plays host to the largest Mid-Autumn lantern carnival in town, with impressive lantern displays, Chinese opera performances and more.](/eng/place-to-go/travel.guide-victoria-park.html)
* [![](/content/dam/dhk/intl/explore/neighbourhoods/interactive-map/attractions/lee-tung-avenue-640x480.jpg)

  Lee Tung Avenue

  Lee Tung Avenue in Wan Chai is a popular spot for photo-taking during the Mid-Autumn Festival with its 200-metre-long walkway lit up by a colourful canopy of lanterns.](/eng/place-to-go/travel.guide-lee-tung-avenue.html)
* [![](/content/dam/dhk/intl/explore/neighbourhoods/interactive-map/attractions/tai-hang-fire-dragon-heritage-centre/tai-hang-fire-dragon-heritage-centre-960x720-b.jpg)

  Tai Hang Fire Dragon Heritage Centre

  This Grade III historic building houses a fascinating exhibition centre that showcases the history of the Tai Hang Fire Dragon Dance and Hakka culture of Tai Hang.](/eng/place-to-go/travel.guide-tai-hang-fire-dragon-heritage-centre.html)
* [![](/content/dam/dhk/intl/explore/neighbourhoods/interactive-map/attractions/fuk-wing-street-toy-street-640x480.jpg)

  Fuk Wing Street

  Also known as ‘Toy Street’, Instagrammers flock to Fuk Wing Street for the kaleidoscopic display of colourful Mid-Autumn lanterns for sale.](/eng/place-to-go/travel.guide-fuk-wing-street.html)
* [![](/content/dam/dhk/intl/explore/neighbourhoods/interactive-map/attractions/tai-yuen-street-toys-640x480.jpg)

  Tai Yuen Street

  Among the treasure trove of toys, you’ll find decorations for various festivals including lanterns for Mid-Autumn.](/eng/place-to-go/travel.guide-tai-yuen-street.html)

An ancient tradition observed in many parts of Asia, the Mid-Autumn Festival celebrates a community’s successful harvest season. It is also known as the ‘moon festival’, as it takes place on the eighth full moon of the lunar calendar.

## What are the customs and traditions?

During this time, you’ll no doubt notice the shops are full of brightly coloured lanterns, which on the night of the festival are typically carried by children, when families go outside to observe the full moon.

In addition, families and friends exchange a traditional treat called mooncakes, a dense pastry with a salted egg yolk in the middle, representing the moon. Mooncakes can also have fillings of lotus paste or red bean paste. The cakes are extremely rich, and you aren’t expected to eat the entire thing; rather it should be cut into wedges and shared.

## What can I see and do?

![Image 4: Chinese lantern for mid autumn festival celebration](https://cdn.cheapoguides.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/7/2019/05/GettyImages-1688665820-770x514.jpg)

The grandest lantern carnival takes place in the evening, at the expansive [Victoria Park](https://hongkongcheapo.com/place/victoria-park/)in Causeway Bay. With massive lantern displays, a cultural program with traditional dances and songs, lion and dragon dances, this is a great opportunity to see and experience the moon festival.

One of the highlights of the lantern carnival in Victoria Park is the incredible [Tai Hang Fire Dragon](https://hongkongcheapo.com/events/tai-hang-fire-dragon/). This is not your typical dragon dance — this 67-metre-long dragon is covered with thousands of burning sticks of incense, and more than 300 people are needed to make the massive beast dance through the streets. The procession starts in Tai Hang village, in Causeway Bay, and ends at Victoria Park, later in the night.

Another prime spot to enjoy lantern displays is over in Tsim Sha Tsui, in front of the [Hong Kong Cultural Centre](https://hongkongcheapo.com/place/hong-kong-cultural-centre/). The open-air piazza by the Clock Tower is transformed into a glowing exhibition of elaborate lanterns.

## Our recommended itinerary

If you’re staying in Kowloon, here is our recommended itinerary for seeing all of the activities on the night of the Mid-Autumn Festival:

*   Start with the lantern carnival in [Tsim Sha Tsui](https://hongkongcheapo.com/locations/kowloon/tsim-sha-tsui/), and admire the views of Victoria Harbour from the waterfront.
*   Jump on the historic Star Ferry to get to Central.
*   Walk to Des Voeux Road (about 10 minutes) and [catch the tram](https://hongkongcheapo.com/getting-around/tour-hong-kong-by-tram/) (ding ding!) going to Causeway Bay.       
*   Get off at Victoria Park, and follow the crowds to the action.

# How to Celebrate Mid-Autumn Festival in Hong Kong 2026?

One of the loveliest Chinese festivals, [the Mid-Autumn Festival](/festivals/mid-autumn-festival.htm), is grandly celebrated every year in Hong Kong.

With traditional Chinese lanterns, parades, and what seems to be an endless supply of mooncakes on offer around Hong Kong, there are plenty of places to celebrate this iconic event. The Mid-Autumn Festival is certainly worth exploring in Hong Kong. Check out our recommendations below to plan the perfect festive night.

## When Is the Mid-Autumn Festival 2026 in Hong Kong?

The Mid-Autumn festival is usually celebrated on the fifteenth day of the Chinese lunar calendar's eighth month. This year, the Mid-Autumn Festival is on September 25th (Friday). The people of Hong Kong will have a public holiday on September 26th.

## Hong Kong's Best Mid-Autumn Festival Events in 2026

### 1. Tai Hang's Fire Dragon Dance

The Tai Hang Fire Dragon Dance will take place in Tai Hang, Causeway Bay, on Hong Kong Island.

The Tai Hang Fire Dragon Parade started mysteriously in 1880 when the village was ravaged by a typhoon and a plague on the eve of the Mid-Autumn festival.

It has become one of the most famous events of celebrating the Mid-Autumn festival in Hong Kong. It is in the popular tourist area of Causeway Bay.

The "fire dragon" is about 67 meters (220 feet) long and is composed of 32 connected sections. It is built from straw and covered with joss sticks, which are lit at night. Every year, it brings thousands of eager spectators to the narrow alleys of Tai Hang.

![mid autumn festival fire dragon dance in hong kong](https://data.chinahighlights.com/grey.gif)

### 2. Mid-Autumn Lantern Carnival at Victoria Park

One old tradition of the Mid-Autumn Festival is to light and hang lanterns. During the period of the festival, there are usually lantern carnivals throughout Hong Kong, but the largest and most popular carnival is found in Victoria Park in Causeway Bay.

Besides the brightly lit lanterns, there is usually a big fire dragon dance, Mid-Autumn Festival markets, traditional handicraft demonstrations, and lantern riddle guessing.

* Event Time: TBC
* Lighting-up Time: 6:30 pm -11 pm (based on last year's arrangement)
* Venues: Soccer Pitches No. 1 -6 (based on last year's arrangement)

### 3. Yuen Long 'Lantern Street'

Ahead of the Mid-Autumn festival, a large number of citizens go to buy their favorite traditional lanterns at 'Lantern Street' in Yuen Long District's Tai Kiu Market. You will see thousands of traditional, handcrafted lanterns, resembling everything from rabbits and goldfish to fruits and other traditional designs.

![mid autumn festival lanterns](https://data.chinahighlights.com/grey.gif)

## Best Restaurants for Mid-Autumn Festival in Hong Kong

![Hong Kong](https://data.chinahighlights.com/grey.gif)

If you are planning a romantic dinner on the evening of the Mid-Autumn festival, Hong Kong has many great restaurants to offer:

**Alto:** Sitting pretty on the top floor of Causeway Bay's V Point building, Alto is a contemporary steakhouse. You can enjoy a meal as you take in the vast Victoria Harbour vista and many of Hong Kong's landmark skyscrapers.

**Felix:** The restaurant is housed on the 28th floor of the Peninsula Hotel and has a magnificent view of Victoria Harbour. It is a dining place at dinner time and changes to a bar afterward.

**Cruise Restaurant & Bar:** Cruise is a rooftop restaurant and bar with an outdoor oasis — a terrace overlooking Hong Kong's Victoria Harbour, serving modern Asian food. "Expect the unexpected!"

Lanterns are the soul of the festival. As a child, I remember making paper lanterns at school, but these days, the sheer variety is mind-boggling. Traditional paper lanterns, humble yet charming, still line local shops; bamboo-framed lanterns shaped like rabbits or starfruit are a nod to fading craftsmanship. Of course, plastic, battery-powered lanterns have taken over, piping out off-key tunes and cartoon jingles. At first, I rolled my eyes, but there’s something endearing about their kitschiness—the city wouldn’t be the same without them. [...] If there’s one must-see, it’s the lantern displays at Victoria Park. Each year, the park transforms into a luminous wonderland, brimming with oversized installations and glowing arches. It’s gloriously tacky, but the infectious local energy makes it an unmissable spectacle. Solving lantern riddles is another tradition—witty, auspicious messages dangle from the lanterns, ready to stump even the most seasoned puzzle-solvers.

Image 7: becoming-carmen-hong-kong-chinese-mid-autumn-festival-lantern-installation-carnival-victoria-park-causeway-bay

### Wax Burning: A Fading Rite of Mischief [...] To close, I always find myself thinking of Su Shi’s “Water Melody”, whose verses so perfectly capture the bittersweet joy of reunion and separation. Its closing line, “但願人長久，千里共嬋娟” (“May we live long and share the moon’s beauty, even if we are a thousand miles apart”), sums up everything I love about Mid-Autumn in Hong Kong—the festival that invites us to look up, reach out, and remember that distance is no barrier to connection.

> “明月幾時有？把酒問青天。不知天上宮闕，今夕是何年？
> 
> 
> 
> 我欲乘風歸去，又恐瓊樓玉宇，高處不勝寒。 起舞弄清影，何似在人間？
> 
> 
> 
> 轉朱閣，低綺戶，照無眠。
> 
> 
> 
> 不應有恨，何事長向別時圓？
> 
> 
> 
> 人有悲歡離合，月有陰晴圓缺，此事古難全。但願人長久，千里共嬋娟。”

— 蘇東坡《水調歌頭》

# There’s More to the Mid-Autumn Festival than just Mooncakes

[Hong Kong](https://www.becoming-carmen.com/destinations/category/Hong+Kong)

6 Oct

Written By [Carmen Ho](https://www.becoming-carmen.com/destinations?author=5484b522e4b08bf981feed21)

![Image 3: becoming-carmen-hong-kong-chinese-mid-autumn-festival-starfruit-rabbit-lanterns-mooncake-candles](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/5fa57aacf5b0a90a76b0d7cc/2d1b5b01-b4d2-4c2b-aadb-c52f4bf4c065/becoming-carmen-hong-kong-chinese-mid-autumn-festival-starfruit-rabbit-lanterns-mooncake-candles)

**Did someone mention mooncakes?**

There’s something uniquely enchanting about Hong Kong during the Mid-Autumn Festival. As the city slips into September, anticipation bubbles beneath the surface—shop windows fill with ornately packaged mooncakes, lantern stalls spring up on street corners, and the air takes on a faint, promising coolness. For me, it’s the most heartfelt of all Chinese festivals—a time when the city slows down just enough for us to notice the moon, reconnect with family, and indulge in a bit of nostalgia.

The Mid-Autumn Festival, also known as the Moon Festival or Mooncake Festival, is a cornerstone of Chinese culture, sitting right up there with Chinese New Year in the local consciousness. It’s Hong Kong’s answer to Thanksgiving, blending gratitude, reunion, and a dash of magic on the brightest full moon night of the year—the fifteenth day of the eighth lunar month, usually somewhere between mid-September and early October. Unlike the rowdy fanfare of Chinese New Year, Mid-Autumn feels more intimate, more local. If you ever wanted to experience authentic Hong Kong, this is the festival that opens a window into its soul.

Fun fact: Since most Mid-Autumn celebrations occur at night, the day after the Mid-Autumn Festival is a public holiday instead of the festival date itself. If the day after the festival falls on a Sunday, the following Monday will be designated a public holiday. Metro services are also extended to run overnight, such as on Christmas Eve and New Year’s Eve.

* * *

## Folklore: Legends Beneath the Moon

No Mid-Autumn Festival is complete without a touch of myth. The most beloved tale tells of Hou Yi, the legendary archer who saved the earth by shooting down nine scorching suns, only to lose his wife Chang’e to the heavens. After consuming the elixir of immortality, she floated up to the moon, where she remains as the Moon Goddess, accompanied by the Jade Rabbit. As a child, I remember being mesmerised by this story, searching for shapes on the moon’s surface and believing, just for a moment, in celestial romance. It’s a motif you’ll see everywhere this season—rabbits and moon maidens, woven into lanterns, cakes, and childhood memories.

* * *

## Traditions

### Mooncake Mania

Let’s not kid ourselves—the real star of the show is the mooncake. These ornate pastries are as much a tradition as they are a culinary adventure, having originated during the Yuan Dynasty in China, where they were used to smuggle messages and plan an uprising on the night of the full moon. With round shapes representing reunion and completeness, their auspicious symbolism is why they are frequently gifted between friends, families, and clients to be enjoyed during the festival. And because of their popularity, mooncake tins often require pre-ordering, just like Chinese New Year puddings.

*   **Traditional mooncakes (傳統月餅)**: The OG (and my favourite), with its thick golden crust, creamy white lotus filling, and salted egg yolks gleaming like miniature full moons (雙黃白蓮蓉), remains my personal favourite. Elaborate designs of the bakery’s name with floral embellishments are often imprinted on the top of the mooncake for decorative purposes, with Maxim’s (美心西餅) and Kee Wah Bakery (奇華餅家) being popular contenders for their consistently lush textures.

*   **Snowy mooncakes (冰皮月餅)**: The innovative delicacy burst onto the scene thanks to Taipan Bakery (which has since sadly closed for business) and is now a cult favourite—think chewy mochi-skin, served cold, with fillings ranging from green bean paste to mango pomelo sago, a beloved local dessert, and even the love-it-or-hate-it durian.

*   **Custard mooncakes (奶皇月餅)**: A fusion of tradition and modernity, custard mooncakes are said to have been inspired by local custard buns. The gift sets from Peninsula Boutique (半島精品店) and Imperial Patisserie (皇玥) are truly in a league of their own; gently warmed, the filling oozes like molten gold, and their packaging is almost too pretty to open. If you’re sending a gift, these are the way to go—trust me, the delight is universal.

### Family Feasts and Moonlit Strolls

Mid-Autumn is all about reunion. Families gather for dinner, often finishing with a platter of pomelo, starfruit, and—of course—mooncakes. The city’s parks come alive after dusk, with families and friends wandering under the full moon. There’s a notable hush in the air, a sense of togetherness that’s hard to describe but impossible to miss. My favourite spots for moongazing? West Kowloon Art Park’s grassy lawns, Tamar Park’s city-meets-sea vistas, and the buzzing Sun Yat Sen Memorial Park. You’ll find people setting up picnics, laying out lanterns, and simply soaking in the glow.

### Lantern Displays: Colour, Craft and Childhood

![Image 4: becoming-carmen-hong-kong-chinese-mid-autumn-festival-lantern-installation-displays-little-tai-hang](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/5fa57aacf5b0a90a76b0d7cc/cd647302-91ad-4315-803e-6cf117ac7aed/becoming-carmen-hong-kong-chinese-mid-autumn-festival-lantern-installation-displays-little-tai-hang)

![Image 5: becoming-carmen-hong-kong-chinese-mid-autumn-festival-lantern-installation-carnival-victoria-park-causeway-bay](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/5fa57aacf5b0a90a76b0d7cc/a9e4d4dc-80c2-40a4-a0d9-e9d048754b1e/becoming-carmen-hong-kong-chinese-mid-autumn-festival-lantern-installation-carnival-victoria-park-causeway-bay)

Lanterns are the soul of the festival. As a child, I remember making paper lanterns at school, but these days, the sheer variety is mind-boggling. Traditional paper lanterns, humble yet charming, still line local shops; bamboo-framed lanterns shaped like rabbits or starfruit are a nod to fading craftsmanship. Of course, plastic, battery-powered lanterns have taken over, piping out off-key tunes and cartoon jingles. At first, I rolled my eyes, but there’s something endearing about their kitschiness—the city wouldn’t be the same without them.

If there’s one must-see, it’s the lantern displays at Victoria Park. Each year, the park transforms into a luminous wonderland, brimming with oversized installations and glowing arches. It’s gloriously tacky, but the infectious local energy makes it an unmissable spectacle. Solving lantern riddles is another tradition—witty, auspicious messages dangle from the lanterns, ready to stump even the most seasoned puzzle-solvers.

![Image 6: becoming-carmen-hong-kong-chinese-mid-autumn-festival-lantern-installation-carnival-victoria-park-causeway-bay](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/5fa57aacf5b0a90a76b0d7cc/69d69d01-94d2-4d69-a1c6-be741c489ffe/becoming-carmen-hong-kong-chinese-mid-autumn-festival-lantern-installation-carnival-victoria-park-causeway-bay)

![Image 7: becoming-carmen-hong-kong-chinese-mid-autumn-festival-lantern-installation-carnival-victoria-park-causeway-bay](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/5fa57aacf5b0a90a76b0d7cc/2d987417-328b-4725-adce-c46ee4bc99d3/becoming-carmen-hong-kong-chinese-mid-autumn-festival-lantern-installation-carnival-victoria-park-causeway-bay)

### Wax Burning: A Fading Rite of Mischief

Ask any local about their childhood Mid-Autumn memories, and wax burning (known as “煲蠟”, literally “boiling wax” in Chinese) will inevitably come up. It started with mooncake gifting, a popular gesture that left families with piles of empty tins at the end of the Mid-Autumn Festival. Before smartphones and computers existed, there were limited forms of entertainment (you could only eat as many mooncakes and stare at the full moon for so long). Youngsters would gather in parks, melting spare candles in tin boxes, sometimes tossing water onto the molten puddles for a burst of flames. It was equal parts thrilling and dangerous—a rite of passage now largely stamped out by safety regulations since the 1990s. I don’t condone it (and neither does the city), but there’s a wistful nostalgia for the wild, unsupervised nights of youth.

_Disclaimer: Wax burning is dangerous and a nightmare to clean up._

### Glow Sticks: A Neon Farewell

With wax burning on the way out, glow sticks became the safer way for kids (and let’s be honest, adults) to play. Thin tubes would be bent into connectors, creating glow-in-the-dark bracelets, flowers, and balls that could be tossed around for fun. Because a limited number of connectors are included in each pack, kids would save and reuse them the following year. I always went for pink and blue (yellow and green were too mainstream, red too dim, and everyone seemed to despise orange), and I used to leave them overnight in my room until they faded into nothing. In 2024, with new plastic bans, glow sticks sparkled in Hong Kong parks for the last time—a bittersweet goodbye to an easy, affordable joy.

### Fire Dragon Dance: Tradition in Motion

![Image 8](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/5fa57aacf5b0a90a76b0d7cc/ea9dedaf-d994-415a-b8e0-1c8971e2e1c0/IMG_3358-squarespace.jpg)

![Image 9](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/5fa57aacf5b0a90a76b0d7cc/b4bde392-0e50-4b86-8f80-021541fcbed0/IMG_3376-squarespace.jpg)

If there’s one spectacle you shouldn’t miss, it’s the Fire Dragon Dance in Tai Hang. This century-old tradition, rooted in Hakka culture, sees a 67-metre dragon, studded with 12,000 burning incense sticks, winding through the streets to the beat of drums. The head alone weighs nearly 50kg, and the dragon is animated by dozens of dancers, swirling through the crowd in a hypnotic, smoky ritual. I’ve been several times, and every visit feels magical—the scent of incense, the pulsing energy, the sense that ancient legends are alive and well in the heart of the city.

* * *

## Popular Sayings

The beauty of the Mid-Autumn Festival lies in its layers: the folklore, food, family, and a city glowing under the moon. The classic greeting, “中秋節快樂” (Happy Mid-Autumn Festival), is on everyone’s lips, while the more poetic “人月兩團圓” captures the longing for reunion as the moon reaches its fullest. For me, the festival is a reminder to pause, to savour the company of loved ones, and to embrace the imperfect beauty of togetherness—whether you’re sharing a mooncake or a quiet moment beneath the sky.

To close, I always find myself thinking of Su Shi’s “Water Melody”, whose verses so perfectly capture the bittersweet joy of reunion and separation. Its closing line, “但願人長久，千里共嬋娟” (“May we live long and share the moon’s beauty, even if we are a thousand miles apart”), sums up everything I love about Mid-Autumn in Hong Kong—the festival that invites us to look up, reach out, and remember that distance is no barrier to connection.

> “明月幾時有？把酒問青天。不知天上宮闕，今夕是何年？
> 
> 
> 
> 我欲乘風歸去，又恐瓊樓玉宇，高處不勝寒。 起舞弄清影，何似在人間？
> 
> 
> 
> 轉朱閣，低綺戶，照無眠。
> 
> 
> 
> 不應有恨，何事長向別時圓？
> 
> 
> 
> 人有悲歡離合，月有陰晴圓缺，此事古難全。但願人長久，千里共嬋娟。”

— 蘇東坡《水調歌頭》
